title: Pershing Square Buys Microsoft, Exits Hilton in Q1 Portfolio Shuffle
body:
In a significant shift in its investment portfolio, Pershing Square Capital Management, the prominent hedge fund led by billionaire Bill Ackman, has made notable changes to its holdings in the first quarter of 2026. According to its latest filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission, the fund has taken a substantial stake in Microsoft, while exiting its position in Hilton and drastically reducing its investment in Alphabet. This move signals a strategic bet on the software giant's potential for long-term growth and a reevaluation of its positions in the hospitality and technology sectors.

The addition of Microsoft to Pershing Square's portfolio is a significant development, given the company's dominant position in the software industry and its ongoing efforts to expand its presence in the cloud computing market. As a core holding, Microsoft is likely to play a key role in the fund's investment strategy, with Ackman and his team presumably optimistic about the company's ability to drive innovation and deliver strong returns. This move also reflects a broader trend of investors seeking to capitalize on the growth potential of the technology sector, particularly in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, which has accelerated the adoption of digital technologies and remote work arrangements.

In contrast, Pershing Square's decision to exit its position in Hilton suggests that the fund is less bullish about the prospects of the hospitality industry, at least in the near term. The pandemic has had a devastating impact on the travel and tourism sectors, and while there are signs of recovery, the industry is likely to face ongoing challenges in the coming months. By selling its stake in Hilton, Pershing Square may be seeking to avoid potential losses or redeploy its capital in more promising areas. This move also highlights the importance of active portfolio management, as investors seek to navigate an increasingly complex and rapidly changing market environment.

The reduction of Pershing Square's stake in Alphabet is another notable development, given the company's status as a technology giant and a leader in the online advertising market. While the fund's decision to slash its investment in Alphabet may suggest some concerns about the company's growth prospects or valuation, it is also possible that Ackman and his team are simply seeking to rebalance their portfolio and manage risk. As a major player in the technology sector, Alphabet remains a significant holding for many investors, and its performance is likely to be closely watched in the coming months.

The changes to Pershing Square's portfolio also reflect the ongoing evolution of the investment landscape, as funds and investors seek to adapt to shifting market conditions and emerging trends. The rise of passive investing and the growing importance of environmental, social, and governance (ESG) factors are just two examples of the factors that are influencing investment decisions and shaping the behavior of market participants. As the investment environment continues to evolve, it will be important for funds like Pershing Square to remain agile and responsive, making strategic adjustments to their portfolios in order to drive returns and achieve their investment objectives.

As the second quarter of 2026 gets underway, investors will be watching Pershing Square's next moves closely, seeking to gain insight into the fund's investment strategy and its outlook for the markets. With its new stake in Microsoft and its reduced position in Alphabet, Pershing Square is poised to navigate the complexities of the technology sector, while its exit from Hilton reflects a more cautious approach to the hospitality industry. As the investment landscape continues to shift and evolve, one thing is clear: Pershing Square remains a major player in the world of finance, and its investment decisions will be closely watched in the months and years to come.
